author: @asificam1
text: I use NiMH cells because they are very leak resistant so I can leave them unattended and not worry much about my devices having leaking battery issues... also their incredible cold weather performance and insane power density compared to other AA cells are also nice to have. But this is still interesting since paper is a wonderful material and NiMH batteries are typically not very cheap to make or flexible... things that paper batteries are good at... though NiMH total cost to own is still cheap since the Japanese made cells are rated to go for 2000 cycles. But still, paper batteries like you show being water activated might be really really good at emergency batteries that are shelf stable until activated by getting them wet.

author: @JK-zl7vv
text:
What if you make a paper battery, and ran a low voltage solar motor with it, that has an offset weight on it, then place that motor on top of a piece of the electrical paper (the paper you showed that produces voltage, by taping on it), and route the wires from the electrical paper, back to your battery paper, do you think it would prevent the battery paper from ever running out of power, since its always putting power back into the battery paper?   You could double the watts going back to the battery paper, by stacking several layers of the electrical paper, that are placed under the vibrating solar motor, this way vibration and gravity are extra input sources that are being utilized, so it does not violate the laws of physics, in fact its using physics to sustain and recapture the losses, that are used to make the motor vibrate.   
   Please try this, I look forward to your results.  🤠👍⭐⭐⭐

author: @leonmccarty9430
text: My pardon, it can also be used to find new elements, with a bank of centrifuges tuned for that atomic weight.

author: @leonmccarty9430
text: You've been speaking a lot about recycling. I had an idea about that for some 15 years now. But do not have the money or the knowledge to bring it into reality. So I think it would be well to offer this Idea to your inner group to work out. I call it the planetary steward. It is theoretically a way of totally recycling everything. It does not need the making of any new equipment, it already exists. Just needs to be put together in a new way. Start by crushing everything into a powder if it is solid, then mix it with a liquid. This combination is sprayed into a blast furnace. turning the spray into vapor. The vapor is pumped into a master centrifuge, which separates the elements down the middle according to the atomic weights. Then the vapor is sent off to other centrifuges that are set for each atomic weight desired. inshore everything can be recycled over and over again indefinitely. Think of it no more worrying about resources ever! The centrifuges are the ones that separate out u 235 from u 238. What separates the yellow cake, after vaporised it. So have a good time working on it, if your group likes it.

replies.author: @NdxtremePro
replies.text: So, assuming you could do this, you are simply breaking everything down to its composite pieces. But maybe you actually want to keep them in their composite forms. This is down in 3D metal printing for things such as stainless steel and titanium by breaking them up into tiny microscopic balls, getting rid of the oxygen as much as possible, and selling the resulting powder as the building material.

author: @marcus3d
text: Wrong. Check your calculations. This has a peak power density of 150 µW/sqcm, which is about 5 W/kg assuming the anode and cathode weigh as much as the paper. A normal li-ion AA battery has a power density of 300 W/kg, which is 60 times higher. Although we really should talk about energy density rather than power density. And the "paper" battery (the paper actually has nothing to do with it, but hey, since we're not caring about truth and reality here I suppose that doesn't matter either) is at its peak power when drawing 0.5 mA! Trying to draw even 1 mA gives 0.00004 W at way less than 0.1 V. This has abysmal energy density. Just ridiculously bad.

author: @comicmania2008
text: Not bad, loved the video, thanks! In the RAF around 1979, there were batteries in survival equipment /kits (such as life raft kits), that had batteries that you submerged into the ocean (seawater). We were told that these were used to run a flashing distress beacon, and used saltwater as an electrolyte. Not very powerful, of course, but it could save your life by alerting search aircraft to where you were!

author: @Xero1of1
text: I'm curious... would you be able to stack these paper batteries together using whatever parallel/series connection you need to power an 18V cordless drill? Is it stackable? Will the higher voltage and current alight the battery? If it does catch fire, will be a big fire? Little fire? No fire? How does it work in cold temperatures (freezing) or hot temperatures (inside an oven)? Can it handle inrush current? Can they be recharged? Or can they be modified in some way to make them rechargeable? It would be an interesting video if you could tackle all these questions...

replies.author: @andygozzo72
replies.text: doubt it'd have enough current capability, as its a variation of the standard zinc carbon type, which have poor current driving ability,  voltage wouldnt be a problem, within limits, as you could connect as many in series as physically possible....charging, very unlikely as zinc carbons cant be,  inrush current? not sure what you mean, except peak current capability? as said, current very poor....

author: @jamest.5001
text: Make the price about $1 more than rechargable batteries, and outlaw the cheap batteries that will last about 1/8 as long as the good batts, and will not even work in some things,  like the dollar tree batteries, it's like they are well used good batteries,   if rechargable batteries sold more maybe the cost would be lower!  I think things like remotes, should be converted from 2x aa or aaa batts, to a single lifepo4,  with a built in micro USB to charge it,  going from about 1.7v peak, x 2 , is about 3.4v surely  going to 3.6-3.7 isn't going to hurt anything,  I think remotes should come with rechargeable batteries to start with, use a micro USB,  , charge it for 20 minutes a year, and be done with polluting 2-3 x a year, just turning channels!!. It would probably last 10+ years, especially if the voltage doesn't go too low, and charged too high, keep it to 3.55v and above 3v!  With a quality cell they would probably outlast the remote 2x in some cases!!  Having a LiFePo pouch cell would be great, especially with a plug to charge it!!

replies.text: @ThinkingandTinkering I started using the phrase water powered battery because of these "water batteries" that are just alkali earth metal batteries or transition metal batteries.  they are not real water batteries.  a real one is powered by the water itself and does not use up its materials as a primary cell

author: @maxkennedy5073
text: Interesting, few plastic tubes of different battery sizes with conductive ends, slight change in the morphology, few mL of electrolyte, push in a pre-rolled dry cell, pop the top on and presto your own AA battery or whatever.  Remove roll when dead, more electrolyte another roll and on your way.  Take the old paper, put in an electrolysis cell recover the zinc and away you go again.  Very nice, bugger off disposables.

author: @jamesross1003

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering

replies.author: @jamesross1003
replies.text: @ThinkingandTinkering Been doing so.  My design so far isn't far from the battery to be honest.  My collectors are different and using 2 different carbons.   Coat one side of the paper with graphite and then impregnate the paper with magnesium citrate.  Allow it to dry.  While it is drying make a copper foil collector by heating it to red hot on a hot plate.  Then flake off the soot.  After paper is dry draw a grid pattern with an ink made from graphene foam on the side opposite the graphite.  Use alligator clips to sandwich it all together with one side of the clips covered with tape.  One of the alligator clips in contact with the backside of the copper plate.  The other to the grid pattern.  Sandwich it all together between plexi-glass to hold it tightly together.  Hold it with the grid side to the sun.  Doesn't produce much, in the micro amperes and low milli volts.  But I didn't figure it really would produce much anyway.  It might even be working as an antenna for all I know as I do not have an o-scope and only a cheap meter.  But it does work.  In crystal form the magnesium citrate has a small piezo effect,  I figured that photons hitting it would produce some current.  I need to try some other arrangements and materials.  I only suggested that to you jokingly as I am working on it currently and hints would be nice.  Have a good day!  And thanks for the reply.  Love your videos!

author: @Palmit_
text: Great vid, again. thank you, my tuppence (two pence) worth.. Whilst rechargeable AA's remain pegged at 1.2 volts, 1.5 volts like Alkaline will continue to dominate. Rechargeables are fine for remote controls with slow, infrequent draw.  Things like passive infra-red detectors on LED lighting units, RFID/ electronic keypad locks (which auctuate via a motor),  wildlife cameras etc.. etc.. the voltage needs to be quick for optimal performace. despite being the same current in most cases.

replies.author: @jochenschrey2909
replies.text: ?? You 'll find rechargeable delivering way higher currents than disposables but they self discharge faster. For occasional light use disposables seem more convenient.

replies.author: @Palmit_
replies.text: @jochenschrey2909 we are at odds lol.  not intentionally. i find with 1.2v the mah (rechargeable) generally, is greater at first.  But slowly gets less and less as discharge occurs. The 1.5v mah tends to be almost constant. i speak from my experience and not from any kind of scientific standpoint.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: I think there will be quite a few reasons for disposables being popular but I get your point mate

replies.author: @jochenschrey2909
replies.text: @Palmit_ ?? once again. "mAh" = capacity, for example number of flashes you 'll get from a charge. I was talking current, "A", formexample recycle speed of the flash gun, performance during quick recharges, like a 4/6/8 minute RC car race

author: @overbuiltautomotive1299
text: Robert great video sir ..The issue i have with off the shelf rechargeable NIMH batterys like AAA and so forth is the lower voltage in some cases hurts performance of what its running but thats that

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: yeah indeed mate - but sometimes you get what you get!

replies.author: @overbuiltautomotive1299
replies.text: @ThinkingandTinkering yes sir reBob ye do.and all the pissin and moaning about it changes nothing it seems 🤐

replies.author: @andygozzo72
replies.text: there are, or at least WERE  nickel zinc batteries with a 1.6v terminal voltage, i'm using some in a digital camera which doesnt like nicads/nimhs due to the low voltage, but eats normal alkaline ones pretty quickly , i got 4 of them some years back , unfortunately 2 have since failed after only a few charge cycles, gone 'open'  maybe this is an issue with them? limited shelf life?  i have no idea as yet if theyre still available, i hope so as would like to get a few more, you have to use a charger designed for them as nicad/nimh type is not advisable as simple types will overcharge them (may be possible to mod them), 'smart' ones likely to reject them as 'bad'

replies.author: @overbuiltautomotive1299
replies.text: @andygozzo72 cool ill look into it thanks
